coredata.dc:title: Insights into CQDs-doped perylene diimide photocatalysts for the degradation of naproxen

coredata.dc:description: Advanced efficacious catalytic technologies have the capacity to enhance the degradation of emerging anthropogenic contaminants. Herein, the supramolecular nanofibers, namely carbon quantum dots doped perylene diimide (PDI/CQDs), was prepared via a one-step acidification polymerization method. The results showed that the rate constants of naproxen (NPX) were of 3.50 times in contrast to pristine PDI. When the doping amount of CQDs on PDI was 2%, the half-life of NPX was only 0.66 min in the PDI/CQDs photocatalytic system. The main active substances in the photocatalytic degradation of NPX were h+, O2 •−, 1O2, H2O2 and •OH. Degradation mainly occurred through four paths, including the decarboxylation reaction, classical Ipso-substitution reaction, electron-dominated reaction, and intramolecular dehydration reaction paths. The obvious inhibitory effects of HA, Cl−, NO2 – and HCO3 – was observed, where a lower pH value was beneficial for the degradation of NPX. An overall mechanism was proposed through Density Functional Theory calculations of high-efficiency electron-hole separation of PDI/CQDs. To improve the nanomaterial applicability, PDI/CQDs cellulose acetate membrane was synthesized and used under different water conditions, which further revealed the stability and efficient of this membrane. Ultimately, the aggregate results provided basic supportive data for the remediation of contaminants in water and wastewater.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Advanced efficacious catalytic technologies have the capacity to enhance the degradation of emerging anthropogenic contaminants. Herein, the supramolecular nanofibers, namely carbon quantum dots doped perylene diimide (PDI/CQDs), was prepared via a one-step acidification polymerization method. The results showed that the rate constants of naproxen (NPX) were of 3.50 times in contrast to pristine PDI. When the doping amount of CQDs on PDI was 2%, the half-life of NPX was only 0.66 min in the PDI/CQDs photocatalytic system. The main active substances in the photocatalytic degradation of NPX were h+, O2 •−, 1O2, H2O2 and •OH. Degradation mainly occurred through four paths, including the decarboxylation reaction, classical Ipso-substitution reaction, electron-dominated reaction, and intramolecular dehydration reaction paths. The obvious inhibitory effects of HA, Cl−, NO2 – and HCO3 – was observed, where a lower pH value was beneficial for the degradation of NPX. An overall mechanism was proposed through Density Functional Theory calculations of high-efficiency electron-hole separation of PDI/CQDs. To improve the nanomaterial applicability, PDI/CQDs cellulose acetate membrane was synthesized and used under different water conditions, which further revealed the stability and efficient of this membrane. Ultimately, the aggregate results provided basic supportive data for the remediation of contaminants in water and wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, the existence of emerging contaminants in the environment can have adverse effects on human physiology [1], e.g., naproxen (NPX [(S)-6-methoxy-α-methyl-2-naphthalene acetic acid]) [2]. The remaining drugs that are not fully removed by wastewater treatment plants will enter various natural water bodies [3,4]. These low concentration NPX emissions have certain inhibitory effects on the photosynthesis of aquatic plants, as well as the growth and reproductive capacities of marine life (e.g., zebrafish) [5,6]. It was revealed that individuals who were subjected to long-term exposure to trace amounts of NPX had a higher risk of heart attack or stroke than those in a control group [7]. Thus, the presence of NPX in natural ecosystems cannot be ignored.

Many advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) (e.g., chlorine dioxide, O3, UV/Cl2, UV/O3, persulfate activation, and UV/H2O2) have been developed [2,8–14] with the aim of eradicating emerging contaminants including NPX, which are currently difficult to remove through traditional sewage treatment methods. Although these potent oxidizing agents can quickly and effectively remove pollutants from water, considerable efforts are required for their production, transportation, and storage, which primarily involve safety risks [15,16]. Consequently, it is critical to develop alternative technologies that are safe and efficacious, with low energy requirements. In this regard, photocatalytic technologies that employ renewable sunlight, or low power/high efficiency visible light lamps to stimulate semiconductor photocatalysts, can generate a series of reactive species with strong oxidation capacities for the eradication of emerging contaminants [17].

Photocatalytic materials have been under development for several generations since the 1970′s, which began with metal oxides (TiO2, ZnO, WO3, etc.) [18–21]; doped metal oxides (typical metal doping [22]; non-metal doping [23], etc.); nonmetallic photocatalysts and their doped substances (typical C3N4 and its derivative photocatalysts [24], etc.); to present day organic supramolecular photocatalysts (such as supramolecular pyrene amide [25]). With the improvement of nanomaterials, scientists have set their focus on the development of photocatalysts with lower toxicity and higher efficiency [26].

Perylene diimide (PDI) and its related materials are good N-type organic semiconductors, which possess π-π conjugation and an internal electric field effect, enabling the migration of electrons to the surfaces of the nanofibers that participate in photocatalytic reactions [25]. To further enhance catalysis, PDI has been doped with metal and non-metal substances to increase electron migration and catalytic activity (e.g., rGO-PDI [25], C3N4-PDI [27], and TiO2-PDI [28]). To date, there are few researches on the extension and enhancement of its spectral absorption. Therefore, the simultaneous improvement of PDI’s electron conductivity, effective light utilization, and catalytic capacity has become more desirable. Carbon based materials are commonly used such as biochar, however, although these materials are widely available, the procedures required to prepare them into suitable materials are complicated and time-consuming, while carbon quantum dots(CQDs) are easy to prepare, high yield, low toxicity, and inexpensive precursor[29]. Therefore, in this study, CQDs were used to modify the PDI. CQDs have broadening light absorption properties, excellent electron conduction ability and upconversion effects. A comparison of carbon quantum dots with other quantum dots is listed in Table S1. Based on the composite theory of PDI photocatalysis [30,31], CQDs with extended spectral functions and high electron conductivity can be employed to PDI structure in the preparation of CQD-doped PDI composite photocatalysts with robust activity [32].

At present, there remains inadequate research regarding these NPX products, including their degradation kinetics and related evolutionary processes, as well as free radical attack mechanisms. Furthermore, the applied basic study of PDI composite photocatalysts is still lacking, which encompasses the formation of a catalytic membrane or a catalytic mesh using PDI powder, sequence batch testing, and the enhancement of contaminant mineralization.

This study investigated the shortcomings above by conducting the following research: 1) PDI supramolecular photocatalyst was prepared via an acidification polymerization method, where the PDI/CQDs nanomaterials were composited for the first time; 2) The PDI/CQDs photocatalyst was employed for the degradation of NPX, and the degradation processes were confirmed through the evolution curves of NPX, 1-(6-methoxy-2-naphthyl)–ethanol (NPX1), and 2-acetyl-6-methoxynaphthalene (NPX2); 3) The degradation kinetics, activities of the photocatalyst under different light sources, quenching of reactive species, and the detection of reactive species concentrations were investigated; 4) The degradation paths of NPX were determined, and the photocatalytic mechanisms, particularly the theoretical model of electron-hole separation were explained by Density Functional Theory (DFT); 5) The numerical analysis for the relationship of non-radicals, environmental factors, and NPX rate constants was studied; 6) The applied basic study of the PDI/CQDs cellulose acetate membrane was conducted in different water matrices under natural sunlight irradiation. This PDI/CQDs catalytic system is expected to be developed as a new solar responsive in-situ environmental remediation technology. Concomitantly, relevant test results will further elucidate the environmental trends of emerging contaminants.

A brief overview of laboratory chemicals and reagents will be provided in Text S1, Supplementary material. For the convenience of reading, the abbreviations of the terms involved in this paper are shown in Table 1 .

Preparation of the pristine PDI and CQDs generally followed the synthetic strategy of Zhu et al. and Qu et al. [33–36], respectively, and improvements to the experimental details were implemented (Texts S2-S3). While the PDI/CQDs were prepared using a one-step strategy for the first time: shortly, the bulk PDI (2.679 g) and different mass ratios (x) of solid CQDs (x = 0%, 1%, 2%, 4%, 10%), respectively, were accurately weighed. The mixture was dissolved in 0.834 ml triethylamine and 27.3 ml 4 M HCl, which was undergone 30 min for ultrasound (2101 TH-40 kHz/100 W, Shanghai Anpu Experimental Technology Co., Shanghai; China) and two hours of a thermostatic water bath at 25 °C (Fig. S1). Subsequently, the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane (Shanghai Anpu Experimental Technology Co., Shanghai; China) and dried at 60 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the PDI/CQDs photocatalysts (PDI, PDI/CQDs1%, PDI/CQDs2%, PDI/CQDs4%, and PDI/CQDs10%) were obtained. Additionally, a PDI/CQDs cellulose acetate membrane was prepared by means of ultrasonic immersion, in which each square centimeter of the membrane contained 0.68 ± 0.04 mg of PDI/CQDs2% (Text S4).

The structural morphologies and photochemical properties of the pristine PDI and PDI/CQDs photocatalysts were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-vis/DRS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and photoluminescence (PL), respectively (detailed manufacturer information and operating parameters showed in Text S5).

Photocatalytic experiments were carried out using a custom lab-made photocatalytic system (schematics shown in Fig. S1), with which the general experiments were conducted under visible light. The light source contained 9 W light-emitting diodes (LED; Epileds Technologies, Taiwan) (blue light, 455–460 nm), and the optical power density of the light source was measured to be 6.2 mW cm−2 using a CEL-NF2000 light power meter (Beijing Zhongjiao Jinyuan Technology Co.; China). Unless otherwise specified, the experimental processes of this study were generally described as follows: 10 mg of the photocatalyst was added to a glass beaker which contained 50 ml of 10 mg L−1 NPX (or NPX1, NPX2) [37]. The suspension was magnetically stirred for 30 min in the dark to ensure adsorption-desorption equilibrium.

Periodically, 1 ml of the suspension was sampled and filtered through a 0.22 μm aqueous filter membrane, and each experiment was repeated at least three times. The residual NPX (or NPX1, NPX2) were detected via Shimazu LC-18A high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC; Japan). Where the test parameters are presented in Text S6 and Table S2. To elucidate the relationships between NPX degradation and NPX1 and NPX2 generation, the experiment proceeded until the target contaminants (NPX, NPX1, and NPX2) had completely degraded.

The kinetics of NPX degradation was studied. The following Eq. is the pseudo-first order equation [37]:

Where C0 is the initial concentration of NPX, mg L−1; C0 is the initial concentration of NPX at time t, mg L−1; kobs is the kinetics apparent rate constant of pseudo-first order reaction, min−1. kobs value is the slope of curve of ln(C/C0) vs time.

To investigate the effects of different light sources, the degradation of NPX, NPX1 and NPX2 was studied under exposure of purple (390–400 nm, 4.0 mW cm−2), blue (455–460 nm, 6.2 mW cm−2), green (515–530 nm, 6.0 mW cm−2), yellow (590–595 nm, 4.0 mW cm−2), and red (655–660 nm, 11.0 mW cm−2) light, respectively. Further, to explore the impacts of environmental factors, different ions and substances (e.g., humic acid (HA), Cl−, NO2 – and HCO3 –, etc.) were added to the working solution prior to conducting the photocatalytic experiments; by adding dilute sulfuric acid or sodium hydroxide to adjust the pH of the working solution, the effects of different species of influencing factors on NPX degradation were studied.

To study the potential application performance of the as-prepared PDI/CQDs2% cellulose acetate membrane, photocatalytic degradation experiments were carried out under natural sunlight irradiation (10:00 a.m. to 16:00 p.m. on sunny days, from April to September in 2021 in Guangzhou, China (N 23.04°, E 113.41°)). The temperature was 29 ± 3 °C; and the light intensity was 42–56 mW cm−2. To identify the effects of real water matrices, tap water, urban river water, lake water, and Pearl River water were filtered and used as solvents for the photocatalytic experiments, which were conducted to investigate the effects of different water matrices on the photocatalytic degradation of NPX by the PDI/CQDs2% cellulose acetate membrane under natural sunlight exposure. Additionally, the successive water treatment processes with the PDI/CQDs2% cellulose acetate membrane was carried out in lake water. Following the initial treatment, the NPX reserve solution was added directly to the reaction solution, which contained ∼10 mg L−1 of NPX prior to the second treatment. The same operation was repeated 10 times and the NPX degradation constants were recorded.

To investigate the transformation products of the photocatalytic system, the reaction solution was sampled (0.5 ml at each time point) and mixed after 40 s, 1 min, and 2 min of light exposure. The mixed sample was detected using a liquid chromatograph-mass spectrometer (Text S7). For the mineralization level study, following a given time interval, 3 ml of the suspension was sampled and filtered, which was then analyzed via a three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum (F97 fluorescence spectrometer, Manufactured by Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., LTD, Shanghai; China). Detailed instrumentational parameters and test conditions can be seen in Text S8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct qualitative and indirect competitive determination of reactive species
ce:section.ce:para:
The participatory mechanisms of reactive species in the photocatalytic systems, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, JES-FA200, Bruker; Germany) techniques were employed to detect the h+, •OH, 1O2, and O2 •−. Chemical quenching experiments were performed to investigate the contribution of active species (Text S9). Subsequently, the concentration of reactive substances was also measured, using furfuryl alcohol as a probe to test for singlet oxygen, the test method was referenced to Ma et al [38] (Eqs. (2) and (3)); using nitrobenzene as a probe to test for hydroxyl radicals, the test method was referenced to Chen, Huang et al, [13,39,40] (Eqs. (4) and (5)); using NBD-Cl as a probe to test for superoxide anion (Fig. S2a) [41]. Similar to this, the testing method of H2O2 typically referred to Li et al. (Fig. S3) [42]. However, the formula for the average generation rate was designed and verified in this study (Eqs. (6) and (7)), with the detailed information available in Texts S10-S13.

The concentrations of non-radicals (e.g., H2O2 and 1O2) were quantified in different water quality environments, and their relationships with the NPX degradation rate constant was analyzed through IBM SPSS (Statistical Product Service Solutions) Statistics 26. In particular, 0.5 ≤ |R2|, these two variables can be thought of as being related; |R2| < 0.5, can be thought of as two basic irrelevant variables. The band gap (Eg) and conduction band (CB) values of the PDI and the PDI/CQDs2% was studied using the following equation (Eqs. (8) and (9)), which was calculated from the UV-vis/DRS spectrum and XPS analysis.

Where α, h, v, and A are the linear absorption coefficient, Planck’s constant, defined constant, and frequency, respectively. In the equation, n depends on the type of optical transition; VB represents the valence band value, and [GAP] is the band gap width of PDI or PDI/CQDs2%.

The density functional theory (DFT) calculations were implemented using the Gaussian 16C.01 software program [45]. All geometric structures of the ground states were optimized by the B3LYP hybrid functional [46] with the double split valence basis set 6-31G(d) [47], whereas the cam-B3LYP/6-31G(d) level [48] was employed to explore the excited state characteristics. In this study, four geometric structures of PDI/CQDs were simulated for further investigation (Fig. S4). The integral equation formalism polarizable continuum model (IEFPCM) [49] was used to consider the solvent effects, and e−-h+ analysis was performed using Multiwfn software [50]. To simplify the visual examination of e− and h+, the Cele and Chole functions are defined as follows (Eqs. (10) and (11)). The functional behavior of Cele and Chole is akin to the Gaussian function, which are highly smooth functions, the value asymptotically approaches zero from the centroid of an e− or h+. The factor A is introduced to normalize Cele and Chole .

CQDs have been proven to possess excellent electron conductivity via their doping materials [29,51,52]. It can be seen from Fig. 1 a that the rounded shapes of CQDs are regular in shape and almost uniform in size. Most CQDs were between 0.8 and 3.6 nm in diameter [35]. Fig. 1b-c show the morphology of the pristine PDI and CQDs doped PDI materials (e.g., PDI/CQDs2% for characterization tests), in which the as-prepared materials were shown to have fiber-like structures following acidification polymerization. The fairly uniform nanofibers were ∼30 nm in diameter and over 100 nm in length [34]. CQDs addition negligibly changed the PDI morphology. As can be seen in Fig. 1d, the TEM image confirmed the doping of CQDs onto the PDI nanofibers, from which the CQDs were embedded within and on the nanofiber surfaces and exhibited uniform distribution, indicating the one-step synthesis method was beneficial for the formation of the PDI/CQDs2%. For the HAADF-STEM images and EDS mapping test, the PDI/CQDs2% was revealed to contain C, N, and O (Fig. 1e–h).

The phase composition of the PDI and PDI/CQDs2% nanofiber photocatalyst were determined via an X-ray diffractometer (XRD). As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, both the PDI and PDI/CQDs2% photocatalysts had high crystallinity with clear peak patterns. The diffraction peaks at 2θ = 9.93°, 14.03°, 19.92°, 22.93°, 25.88° can be attributed to PDI and PDI/CQDs2% [53,54]. According to the previous study, the marked P0 (2θ = 14.03°) and P1 (2θ = 25.88°) peaks were selected, as the IP1/IP0 strength ratios have commonly been used to evaluate the d-spacing and π-π stacking of catalysts [55,56]. The larger the ratio prior to and after doping, the stronger the π-π stacking and the higher the crystallinity [56]. As the results shown in Table S3, the IP1/IP0 ratio of PDI was 0.97, which increased to 1.05 when the CQDs were added. This may indicate that the PDI/CQDs2% photocatalyst formed more ordered π-π stacking and a smaller π-π d layer distance [56]. This phenomenon caused electron delocalization in the high-density overlapping electron cloud, which then contributed to the migration of carriers.

For the UV-vis/DRS results (Fig. 2b), the maximum absorption boundary of PDI was ∼734 nm and for PDI/CQDs2% was 746 nm. The absorption boundary of the PDI/CQDs2% became slightly wider, which verified the extended optical effect of the CQDs. Compared with traditional photocatalysts P25 (∼320 nm), BiPO4 (∼300 nm), W2O5 (∼405 nm), ZnO (<400 nm), and g-C3N4 (∼450 nm), the absorption spectrums of the PDI and PDI/CQDs2% photocatalyst showed an obvious red shift, which enhanced visible light absorption. According to the bandgap calculation formula (Eqns. 7–8), the PDI and PDI/CQDs2% was 1.61 eV and 1.53 eV, respectively (Fig. 2b inset). Briefly, the UV-vis/DRS test indicated that these supramolecular nanofibers expected to exhibit excellent activity under visible and natural sunlight irradiation (i.e., 390–746 nm).

To further investigate the chemical interactions between the CQDs and PDI supramolecular nanofibers, FTIR spectrometry was employed (Fig. 2c). The results revealed that the surfaces of the CQDs contained abundant O–H and –COOH groups; however, due to the small quantity of doping, these typical groups were not significant in the PDI/CQDs2%. For the PDI and PDI/CQDs2%, the absorption peaks at 610–1040 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 belonged to σC-H and C–H vibrations, respectively. These corresponded to the out-of-plane and in-plane bending vibrations of aromatic rings. The absorption peaks at 1040–1260 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1 corresponded to -N-CH2 and -N-C = O vibrations, respectively. This was primarily due to the introduction of amides and β-alanine into the PDI nanofibers during the polymerization process. There was a strong infrared vibration zone in the range of from 1450 to 1590 cm−1, which was due to the typical σC = C group stretching vibrations. Both the PDI and PDI/CQDs2% exhibited sharp bands centered at ∼1670 cm−1 and ∼1700 cm−1, related to the asymmetry of C = O and the symmetric stretching frequency. The absorption peak at 1700 cm−1 was a carboxylic acid group, while another C = O group at 1670 cm−1 was designated as a functional ketone group. The absorption peak corresponding to 2900–3470 cm−1 was C–H, whereas the absorption peak at ∼3480 cm−1 represented the vibration of O–H [33,34]. In addition, the Raman vibration peaks at 1585 cm−1 and 1289 cm−1 correspond to the C = O and C = C/C–C stretching vibrations in the perylene core structure and C–H vibration, respectively (Fig. 2d).

Similarly, these interaction and functional group tests were consistent with the XPS results (Fig. 2e, S5). Additionally, for XPS-VB, following doping it was observed that the VB of PDI was 1.24 eV and the VB of PDI/CQDs2% was 1.15 eV (Fig. 2f). Overall, the structures, morphologies, and absorption spectra of the PDI and PDI/CQDs2% nanofibers confirmed the success of the acidification polymerization preparation techniques through the addition of CQDs, highlighted the potential application of these photocatalysts under visible and natural sunlight.

The as-prepared nanofibers were employed for the photocatalytic degradation of NPX and its main products NPX1 and NPX2. As can be seen from Fig. 3 a and b, the NPX concentration (C/C0) was altered relative to the irradiation time under different catalytic CQDs doping ratios. However, the degradation of NPX by CQDs alone under the same conditions was negligible (Fig. S6). Without the addition of catalysts, the NPX removal rate was negligible and the direct photolysis rate was 2.17×10−6 mol L−1 min−1 (zero-order rate), indicating that the NPX decomposition was recalcitrant under visible light irradiation, which could be ignored compared with the catalytic degradation process [57]. In addition, the adsorption of different CQDs doping ratios was not remarkable, from which the addition of CQDs slightly hindered the adsorption of the materials. It was obvious that the PDI-based nanofibers significantly promoted the degradation of NPX. Compared to the adsorption effect, the photocatalytic degradation process significantly enhanced the removal of NPX. Besides, when the initial concentration of NPX was increased from 1 mg L−1 to 50 mg L−1, the photocatalytic effect of PDI/CQDs2% was weakened (Fig. S7). This may have been due to the high concentration of NPX that absorbed part of the incident light, which reduced the number of photons activating PDI/CQDs2%, thus decreasing the degradation rate of NPX.

For the PDI, PDI/CQDs1%, PDI/CQDs2%, PDI/CQDs4% and PDI/CQDs10%, the degradation rate constants of NPX were 0.614, 1.432, 2.138, 1.131, and 0.918 min−1 (pseudo first-order rate), respectively. Consequently, the existence of PDI-based nanofibers significantly improved the NPX degradation rate in contrast to photolysis and adsorption. Notably, the doped catalyst (e.g., PDI/CQDs2%) completely degraded the NPX within 2 min, from which the rate constant was almost 3.5 times that of its pristine counterpart. The results of doping experiments revealed that the CQDs effectively improved the activity of the PDI/CQDs photocatalyst, which can quickly degrade NPX.

When the doping rate was increased from 0% to 2%, the degradation of NPX exhibited an upward trend. However, when the mass ratio was further increased to 4% and 10%, the degradation rate decreased, which may have been caused by the hindering of the π-π stacking effect of PDI (Fig. 2a). According to the kinetic results, it was found that the optimal doping ratio of the CQDs was 2%. Therefore, unless otherwise specified, the PDI/CQDs2% was used to characterization, photocatalytic activity, photocatalytic mechanism, degradation paths, and applied basic study.

As shown in Fig. 3c, the PDI/CQDs2% photocatalyst showed good activity under purple, blue, green, yellow and red light irradiation. Under these experimental light sources, the rate constants were 1.154, 2.138, 0.809, 0.716, and 0.900 min−1, respectively, all of which were larger than the rate constant of the PDI. The results indicated that the PDI/CQDs2% exhibited improved photocatalytic NPX degradation, highlighting the operability of the CQDs-doped PDI.

Moreover, according to the HPLC spectrum, standard samples, and residence time, the NPX1 and NPX2 products were confirmed (Fig. S8). Compared with the photocatalytic activity of PDI materials, the degradation rate constant of NPX1 by PDI/CQDs2% increased from 0.0181 min−1 to 0.0367 min−1. For NPX2, the degradation rate constant increased from 0.00435 min−1 to 0.009 min−1(Fig. 3d). These results were consistent under the different experimental light sources and evolution processes of NPX1 and NPX2 (Fig. 3e-f), from which the highest generated concentration of NPX1 (∼0.08 h) and NPX2 (∼0.5 h) in the PDI/CQDs2% photocatalytic system appeared earlier than for the PDI. In addition, the complete degradation times for the main products (NPX1 and NPX2) were ∼10 h under the PDI/CQDs2% system and ∼15 h under the PDI system. The results above further demonstrated that the photocatalytic performance of the PDI was effectively improved following its doping with CQDs.

Importantly, the PDI/CQDs photocatalytic system had the capacity to rapidly treat NPX polluted water. In the optimal doping ratio of CQDs, the half-life of NPX was only 0.66 min in the PDI/CQDs photocatalytic system. For preliminary comparison, many NPX degradation rate constants were obtained from the literatures (Fig. 4 ) [58]-[59]. Notably, this study only provided statistical analysis of existing data and there were still some uncertainties in the current study, e.g. the inconsistent experimental conditions. In addition, some commonly used photocatalysts were also tested synchronously under the same photocatalytic conditions of this study (red points). The photodegradation of NPX under different conditions are summarized in Table S4. Overall, it can be seen from the average rate constant data in the boxplot diagram that the PDI/CQDs photocatalyst had a better photocatalytic effect for the treatment of NPX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of reactive species and contribution rate
ce:section.ce:para: EPR was performed to elucidate the reactive species in the PDI and PDI/CQDs2% photocatalytic systems. As shown in Fig. S9, four types of EPR signals were observed under visible light exposure. The signal intensities of DMPO-•OH, DMPO-O2 •−, and TEMP-1O2 were recorded with signals of 1:2:2:1, 1:1:1:1, and 1:1:1, respectively (Figs. S9a–c). As seen in Fig. S9d, the signal ratio recorded by the two materials were 1:1:1, which represented the TEMPO+ generated through the h+ oxidation reaction and TEMPO. These results confirmed that •OH, O2 •−, 1O2 and h+ existed in both the PDI and PDI/CQDs2% photocatalytic systems.

As depicted in Figs. S9a–c, the signal intensities of three trapping agents increased with a prolonged irradiation time (e.g. 10 min); thus, the •OH, O2 •−, and 1O2 could be continuously generated during the photocatalytic process. Obviously, the doped materials (PDI/CQDs2%) were more capable of producing reactive oxygen species, which was attributed to the addition of CQDs. As seen in Fig. S9d, the intensity of photogenerated h+ gradually weakened, indicating that h+ was progressively depleted due to its direct participation in the photocatalytic degradation [33,60]. Moreover, it could be seen that the signal intensity of the PDI/CQDs2% was weaker than its pristine counterpart. As mentioned above, the valence band of the PDI/CQDs2% was lower than that prior to doping; thus, the strength and oxidation performance of h+ from the doped material was reduced in contrast to the pristine equivalent. Nevertheless, which reactive species (e.g., •OH, O2 •−, 1O2 and h+) that dominated the reaction should be further investigated.

The contribution rates of the reactive species in the reaction system were evaluated using quenching agents (Figs. S10a and b). For the control, IPA (for quenching •OH), TEMPO (for quenching O2 •−), NaN3 (for quenching 1O2 and •OH), Na2C2O4 (for quenching h+) and catalase (for quenching H2O2), the NPX degradation rate constants were 2.138, 2.490, 0.886, 1.045, 0.650, and 1.357 min−1, respectively. These results suggested that the contribution rate of these reactive species followed h+ > O2 •− > 1O2 > H2O2 > •OH. Among them, h+, O2 •− and 1O2 facilitated the removal of NPX. Interestingly, the addition of IPA as well as methanol and tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) slightly promoted the degradation of NPX, which was similar to the study of Wang et al. (Fig. S11) [61]. Likewise, several researchers examined the promotion mechanisms of alcohols, where Zhang found that consumption of photogenerated h+ by alcohol was more efficient than that of water, and the photogenerated current was enhanced [62]. However, the promotion mechanism of IPA was not clear. Wang et al. found that in capturing hydroxyl radicals, alcohols can promote the generation of hydrogen peroxide, and these generated hydrogen peroxide, in turn, generate hydroxyl radicals in the presence of light[63]. In this study, we investigated the mechanism of alcohols promoting NPX degradation by using IPA as a research object.

Toward further elucidation, the reactive species were quantitatively determined in the presence of IPA (Figs. S10c–f). In this study, the concentration of •OH improved from 2.64×10−13 M to 2.98×10−13 M with/without the IPA. For 1O2, the concentration was enhanced from 7.08×10−13 M to 7.92×10−13 M for the PDI/CQDs2% photocatalytic system. For H2O2, the calculated rate constants ranged from 5.51×10−6 M min−1 to 5.88×10−6 M min−1. Thus, the addition of IPA clearly improved the concentration of •OH, H2O2, and 1O2 in the reaction system [63]. However, the existence of IPA prevented the generation of O2 •− for both PDI and PDI/CQDs2% systems. This phenomenon suggested that the promoting effect of NPX degradation through the increased production of •OH, 1O2, and H2O2 may have been more important than the inhibitory effect through the reduced generation of O2 •−. Additionally, more complex organic radicals may have been produced in this reaction system (e.g., CH3C(O)OO•) [64].

Emerging contaminants such as NPX typically transform to a series of intermediate products (e.g., NPX1 and NPX2) prior to mineralization. To explain this degradation mechanism, the UPLC-MS/MS was applied to identify the formation of intermediate products. From the results, the secondary ion mass spectrometry of 16 major products (P1-P16) were accurately detected (Table S5 and Fig. S12). The common feature was that the side chains of the carboxy, particularly C11, C13, and O14 possessed higher electron cloud densities that were attacked by reactive species, which resulted in the degradation of the NPX [65,66]. It was found in MS/MS fragments that NPX and most of the transformation products (e.g., P6, P9, P10, P14, and P15) contained a fragment of m/z = 186 (P14), which indicated that the retention part of the NPX group could be seen in these compounds.

As shown in Fig. 5 , four primary transformation paths were deduced. Path A was the decarboxylation reaction, where the •OH initially attacked O14 to produce a dehydro NPX free radical, which was further attacked by the •OH to produce P14 (m/z = 186) [59]. The subsequent hydroxylation reaction resulted in the generation of NPX1. After the attack by 1O2 or O2 •−, NPX1 underwent dehydroxylation to form NPX2 (m/z = 200). NPX2 has been reported frequently in the literature [32,67,68], which can be attacked by e− in the reaction system to further produce P8 (m/z = 186) and P12 (m/z = 158) via demethylation and a side chain reaction, respectively.

Path B was the classic Ipso-substitution reaction, in which •OH attacked the Ipso aromatic C atom to generate a stable carbon-centered free radical. Following dealkylation, the ether bond was cleaved, which resulted in the formation of P11 (m/z = 174) [69], and a similar Ipso-substitution reaction formed P4 (m/z = 160). Path C belonged to an electron-dominated reaction, where e− attacked the carbon atom with •OH attached, which resulted in a free radical with a naphthol structure. This free radical was then attacked by hydrogen ion to produce 2-Naphthol (P2, m/z = 144). 2-Naphthol is difficult to detect under neutral and alkaline conditions; however, it was obviously produced and confirmed under acidic conditions [70] which was then attacked by e− to form naphthalene radicals. Akin to the above reaction, they were further attacked by hydrogen ions to form P1 (m/z = 128).

Path D was an intramolecular dehydration reaction, where NPX1 was intramolecularly dehydrated to form P3 and further attacked by •OH, following to the Ipso-substitution reaction to form P16 (m/z = 170) [32]. According to an electron reduction reaction, P5 (m/z = 154) was derived from P16. Overall, the transformation of NPX under the PDI/CQDs2% photocatalytic system followed the attack law of reactive species, in which •OH, 1O2 and O2 •− attack paths were prominent, and essentially consistent with the quenching and EPR results. Although h+ played important roles in the degradation, it was clearly not involved in the derivation of product paths, which may have been related to the limitations of current detection methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of environmental factors and non-radical studies
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of common environmental factors in water, such as HA, Cl−, NO2 –, and HCO3 – and the influence of pH on the degradation of NPX under the PDI/CQDs2% photocatalytic system were studied (Fig. 6 and Fig. S13). As was observed, except for pH, most environmental factors (e.g., HA, Cl−, NO2 –, and HCO3 –) inhibited the degradation process with increased concentrations, may due to their rapid quenching effects on •OH and O2 •−, which could be summarized as the mechanism that inhibited the oxidation reaction [34]. For example, the second order rate constants of some common environmental factors in water with •OH are (8.6 ± 0.3) × 106 M−1s−1 to (4.3 ± 0.4) × 109 M−1s−1; O2 •− are (2.4 ± 0.3) × 106 M−1s−1 to (8.1 ± 0.3) × 109 M−1s−1 [71].

As to the influence of increased pH, the NPX degradation rate slowed down, which indicated that the PDI/CQDs2% system possessed stronger photocatalytic activity under acidic conditions. This phenomenon was similar to that of the PDI-based composites [34,55,72], mainly due to more stable π-π bonds under acidic conditions, which was conducive to rapid electron transfer. To more comprehensively elucidate the relationships between environmental factors and the reactive species, the generation and consumption of two other types of reactive species, namely non-radicals (H2O2 and 1O2) were investigated.

Both HA and HCO3 – have strong and significant relationships with H2O2 and 1O2. With the increased concentration of environmental factors, these two non-radicals exhibited an obvious downward trend. For HA, the correlation coefficients (R2) of kfH2O2 and [HA], kfH2O2 and kNPX, kfH2O2 and [1O2]ss, [1O2]ss and [HA], and [1O2]ss and kNPX were −0.692, 0.995, 0.970, −0.529, and 0.989, respectively. For HCO3 –, the correlation coefficients (R2) of kfH2O2 and [HCO3 –], kfH2O2 and kNPX, kfH2O2 and [1O2]ss, [1O2]ss and [HCO3 –], and [1O2]ss and kNPX were −0.945, 0.893, 0.921, −0.865, and 0.961, respectively. As for Cl−, low concentrations promoted the formation of the two non-radicals, while high concentrations inhibited it, which might have been related to the complex reaction mechanism of H2O2 and 1O2 [73].

The pH and NO2 – had little impact on the average H2O2 generation rate. However, NO2 – had a significant inhibitory effect on 1O2, where R2 of [1O2]ss and [NO2 –] was −0.790, and [1O2]ss and kNPX was 0.955. In addition, with increased pH, 1O2 revealed an upward trend and maintained a certain plateau, whereas the kfH2O2 and [1O2]ss presented a significant correlation of R2 = 0.930. The degradation kinetics of NPX at different initial pH levels after the addition of PDI/CQDs2% is shown in Fig. S14, and the degradation rate of NPX continues to decrease with increasing pH. The change in pH value mainly affects the surface charge of the photocatalyst and the contaminant, which further determines the interface between the two. The surface charge of PDI/CQDs2% was measured with a zeta potential analyzer and its zero potential point was calculated to be 1.6 (Fig. S14). This means that for pH < 1.6, the PDI/CQDs2% surface is positively charged, while for pH > 1.6, the PDI/CQDs2% surface is negatively charged. The dissociation constant (pKa) of NPX was previously reported to be 4.19. When the initial 3< pH <4.19, NPX is not dissociated and PDI/CQDs2% is less positively charged, which promotes little degradation; when pH > 4.19, NPX is negatively charged and PDI/CQDs2% is negatively charged, and when pH continues to rise, the repulsion between the catalyst and the contaminant becomes stronger and stronger, thus inhibit the degradation reaction. In short, common environmental factors had significant influence on the photocatalytic system, primarily the quenching reactive species (e.g., •OH, O2 •−, H2O2, and 1O2). However, several reactive species typically transformed with each other, resulting in strong correlational relationship. Thus, quenching one of them often affected the production and contribution of the other, or several other reactive species. This study expressed the influence of environmental factors only on the apparent generation of rate constants, or the influence of apparent concentrations of reactive species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic mechanism of the PDI/CQDs photocatalytic system
ce:section.ce:para: Derived from the above results, a potential photocatalytic mechanism for the degradation of NPX with the PDI/CQDs under visible light irradiation was proposed. As a supramolecular organic semiconductor structure, the PDI formed an H-type π-π stack of nanofibers, which were excited by visible light to generate photogenerated e− and h+. However, due to the unique characteristics of Frenkel excitons produced by organic semiconductors with lower carrier mobilities and higher exciton binding energies, the photocatalytic activities of supramolecular organic systems needed to improve [74]. After the introduction of CQDs, the effective electron conduction property of CQDs accelerated the reaction of photogenerated e− and dissolved O2 to form O2 •− (CB < −0.33 eV), which may derive other reactive species.

To investigate the mechanisms, their transformations were confirmed through the detection of •OH, O2 •−, H2O2, and 1O2 concentrations under the ambient air and oxygen enriched conditions. It can be seen from Figs. S15a and b that the generation rate constants of H2O2 became faster under oxygen enriched conditions (9.68×10−6 M min−1) compared with those of ambient air (5.62×10−6 M min−1). Furthermore, the concentrations of •OH, O2 •− and 1O2 were 3.11×10−13 M, 4.26×10−5 M and 1.06×10−12 M under oxygen enriched conditions, and 2.64×10−13 M, 2.77×10−5 M and 7.08×10−13 M under ambient air conditions, which indicated that oxygen enriched conditions were favorable for the generation of reactive species. Consequently, it was speculated that the production of •OH, O2 •−, H2O2 and 1O2 was related to O2 in water, from which these reactive species were almost formed at the conduction band position of the catalyst (Eqs. (14) to (18)) [65,73,75].

Furthermore, the separation of e− and h+ prior to and following the combination of CQDs and PDI was calculated through DFT (Eqs. (10) to (13)). To engage a deeper investigation into the photoinduced carrier generation mechanism of the PDI/CQDs, four PDI/CQDs models (PDI/CQDs-A, PDI/CQDs-B, PDI/CQDs-C, and PDI/CQDs-D) (Fig.S4) were developed to explore the e− excitation characteristics of the PDI and PDI/CQDs.

The contour maps of Cele and Chole (red isosurfaces, yellow isosurfaces) (Fig. 7 and Fig. S16) for the pristine PDI almost completely overlapped, which verified the presence of localized excitation and that the separation of e− and h+ was quite poor. However, the clearly separated contour maps of Cele and Chole for the PDI/CQDs indicated that charge-transfer excitation was prominent. Additionally, the separation of e− and h+ for the PDI/CQDs was obviously better than that for the pristine PDI. Correspondingly, the effect of photogenerated e− and h+ on the separation efficiency was further investigated by means of transient photocurrent response and photoluminescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 7(e and f), under visible light irradiation, the photocurrent density of the as-prepared samples remained stable during cycling [76]. Significant enhancement of photocurrent and significant reduction of fluorescence emission intensity can be observed by CQDs doping, which confirmed that the photoexcited charge recombination was greatly inhibited and the charge transfer was effectively improved. Thus, the doped CQDs constrained the recombination of e− and h+, while promoting the derivation of reactive species.

Overall, the addition of CQDs was beneficial for the optical expansion performance of the PDI, as well as the effective separation of photogenerated e− and h+ in the photocatalytic system, through which the photocatalytic activity was significantly improved. These two effects promoted the generation of reactive species and further accelerated the rapid degradation of NPX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applied basic study for PDI/CQDs2% cellulose acetate membrane
Degradation of NPX in different water matrices under natural sunlight exposure
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the preliminary application of the prepared materials and overcome the limitations of powdered materials, a preliminary study on the formation of a PDI/CQDs2% cellulose acetate membrane was carried out for this study (section 2.1). The newly prepared membrane material was used to remediate NPX in different types of surface water under natural sunlight irradiation. As shown in Fig. 8 b, the rate constant of NPX in pure water, tap water, urban river water, lake water, and Pearl River water is 0.1049, 0.0675, 0.0498, 0.0502, and 0.0461 min−1, respectively. The results revealed that the rate constants of NPX in tap water, urban river water, lake water, and Pearl River water decreased significantly compared with pure water, with inhibition rates of 35.65%, 52.53%, 52.14%, and 56.05%, respectively.

This was because environmental factors in surface water can quench reactive species in the PDI/CQDs2% photocatalytic system, which further inhibits the photocatalytic degradation of NPX. Among them, the concentration of environmental factors (e.g., Cl− and HCO3 –) (Table S6) in urban river water, lake water, and Pearl River water were higher, and the corresponding rate constants were slower than that of the tap water. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic membrane still exhibited relatively desirable photocatalytic activities for the remediation of NPX in different water matrices, indicating that the PDI/CQDs2% had promising application prospects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Successive water treatment processes using PDI/CQDs2% cellulose acetate membrane
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the performance of the photocatalytic membrane, a successive water treatment process was simulated with lake water under natural sunlight exposure for this study. There was no obvious decline between the 2nd to 5th test (Fig. 8b), with declining rates of 0.99%, 1.59%, 1.79%, and 4.37%, respectively. However, from the 6th to 10th test, the rate constants exhibited a certain downward trend. Compared with the 1st test, the declining rates were 12.90% to 28.37%, respectively. Similarly, the steady-state concentrations of each reactive species changed significantly in subsequent reaction tests (Fig. 8c-d). It was clear that the concentrations of •OH, O2 •− and 1O2 diminished with the increased number of tests, which confirmed the important roles of the reactive oxygen species and intrinsic mechanism of the slower reaction rate.

Interestingly, the average rate constant of H2O2 increased with the greater number of tests, from 1.74×10−7 M min−1 (1st test), to 3.38×10−7 M min−1 (5th test), and 3.34×10−7 M min−1 (10th test), respectively. Consequently, the results further verified that for the continuous degradation of NPX pollutants, the green chemical oxidant H2O2 can be effectively accumulated, which had the benefit of subsequent processing (e.g., Fenton method). In addition, the surface color of the PDI/CQDs2% cellulose acetate membrane became slightly lighter (see inset); thus, it was speculated that the membrane underwent a certain degree of aging following the treatments, albeit no obvious nanoparticles were observed in solution following the 10th reaction. However, even after the last reaction, the membrane retained ∼70% of its initial degradation activity. In short, after simulated water treatment processes, the PDI/CQDs2% cellulose acetate membrane had good cycling and successive reaction performance; thus, it may be applied for the remediation of emerging contaminants (such as NPX) in actual environmental water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of mineralization in NPX degradation process
ce:section.ce:para: The mineralization of NPX was evaluated during its degradation using the PDI/CQDs2% cellulose acetate membrane under natural sunlight irradiation. Three-dimensional fluorescence was employed to determine the residual substances in solution during the photocatalytic NPX degradation process. Prior to the experiment, a background value analysis was performed on the pure water and PDI/CQDs2% cellulose acetate membrane (Fig. 9 ), through which almost no fluorescence signal was detected. As can be seen in Fig. 9 the NPX has a high intensity fluorescence signal in the Ex240-340 nm/Em340-390 nm. It was difficult to decompose the NPX to other products under dark conditions following the addition of the catalyst, which was consistent with the liquid chromatography detection results during the photocatalytic performance tests. However, the product formation and evolution processes could be clearly observed in light condition.

Under sunlight exposure, the three-dimensional fluorescence image of the NPX showed two new obvious signals. With an extended illumination time (1 h) the NPX intensity weakened, which indicated that the NPX was degraded. Simultaneously, a strong signal appears in the range of from Ex260-360 nm/Em400-500 nm, which belonged to the NPX degradation products. Except for some major products that were similar in structure to the parent, there were also many low-molecular-weight acids [77,78]. According to the literature, Ex240-260 nm/Em420-450 nm and Ex285-330 nm/Em400-450 nm can be attributed to fulvic acid-like fluorescence and humic acid-like fluorescence, respectively [79]. Furthermore, longer photoperiods effectively degraded the products and the fluorescence signal of the NPX contaminated water disappears thoroughly, which indicated a high level of mineralization. These results demonstrated that although the mineralization time was much longer than for the degradation of NPX, effective mineralization can still be obtained by prolonging the reaction time.

For this study, CQDs doped PDI was employed to degrade NPX and its main products under visible light. The addition of CQDs enhanced electron transfer, promoted the separation of photogenerated e− and h+, and effectively enhanced the degradation of NPX. These main effects indirectly increased the generation of reactive species, the main contributors to the system included h+, O2 •−, 1O2, H2O2 and •OH. Following the reactive species attack mechanism, the NPX degradation process could be divided into the decarboxylation reaction, classical Ipso-substitution reaction, electron-dominated reaction, and intramolecular dehydration reaction paths. Lower pH value was beneficial for improving the NPX degradation rate constant, while HA, Cl−, NO2 – and HCO3 – showed obvious inhibitory effects. The PDI/CQDs cellulose acetate membrane has high catalytic activity in water remediation, and the effective mineralization can be obtained by prolonging the reaction time; and the photocatalytic membrane had excellent cycling and successive reaction property. These results provided supportive data for the remediation of contaminants in ambient water.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Fifteen texts of the reagents, preparation method and characterization; determination of contaminants concentration, reactive species and transformation products. Six tables of the photocatalytic activity of graphic quantum dots-based materials, HPLC parameters, XRD information, removal efficiency of NPX by different processes, products information and water quality conditions. Sixteen figures of the preparation process, experimental apparatus, standard curve of reactive species, characterization results of XPS determination, effect of CQDs and NPX initial concentrations, liquid chromatographic diagrams, EPR spectra, quenching experiments, total ion current chromatogram, Zeta potentials, reactive species concentrations, and contour map of Cele and Chole . Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.138571.
